explanation_md: After the move, `a.val` is nullptr so its destructor prints nothing. `b.val` still points to the integer 7, so when `b` destructs it prints `destroy 7`. Only one destruction message appears.

prompt_md: Why should a move constructor be marked `noexcept`?

options.a: It is a language requirement — the program will not compile without it
options.b: It allows std::vector and other containers to use the move constructor during reallocation instead of falling back to copy
options.c: It prevents the destructor from being called on the moved-from object
options.d: It makes the move constructor run faster at runtime

explanation_md: Containers like `std::vector` check if the move constructor is `noexcept` before choosing it during reallocation. Without `noexcept`, the container must use the copy constructor to guarantee strong exception safety, defeating the purpose of move semantics. It is not a language requirement (option a is wrong) and has no effect on destruction (option c is wrong).

prompt_md: After executing `Box b = std::move(a);` using a correct move constructor, what is the state of `a`?

options.b: a still holds a valid copy of the original data
options.c: a is in a valid but empty (null pointer) state — do not dereference it
options.d: a and b share ownership of the same resource

explanation_md: A correct move constructor nulls the donor's pointer. `a` remains alive (its destructor still runs at the end of scope) but its internal pointer is null. The moved-from object is valid to destruct or reassign — just not to use for its original purpose. Option d would describe `shared_ptr`.

options.a: The move constructor should use `const Buffer&&` as its parameter type
options.b: The donor's `data` pointer is not nulled after stealing, so both objects delete the same memory
options.d: The destructor should check `if (data)` before calling `delete[]`

explanation_md: After `data(o.data)`, both `this->data` and `o.data` point to the same allocation. When both destructors run, `delete[] data` is called twice on the same pointer — undefined behavior (typically a crash). The fix is to add `o.data = nullptr; o.size = 0;` at the end of the move constructor. Option d is unnecessary because `delete[] nullptr` is already safe.

The compiler applies copy/move elision (NRVO): the object constructed inside `make()` is constructed directly into `t` in the caller. No copy or move constructor is called. Only the default constructor prints `C`. This is guaranteed in C++17 for prvalues (mandatory copy elision).
